title: Kubetail: Aggregate Logs from Multiple Pods
description:
Learn to use Kubetail to aggregate and follow logs from multiple Pods simultaneously.

**Focus**: Installing and using the kubetail tool to tail logs from multiple Pods in one stream.

Kubetail is a bash script that enables you to aggregate (tail/follow) logs from multiple Pods into one stream. This is the same as running `kubectl logs -f` but for multiple Pods at once, which is incredibly useful when you have replicated Pods and want to see all their logs together.

**Why Kubetail?**
- View logs from multiple Pods in a single stream
- Color-coded output to distinguish between different Pods
- Support for multiple containers within Pods
- Regex pattern matching for Pod names
- Namespace filtering

**Use Cases**:
- Monitoring replicated applications (3 nginx Pods, etc.)
- Debugging distributed systems
- Following logs across microservices
- Observing rolling updates in real-time
steps: Install kubetail on your system (MacOS via brew or Linux via git clone)
Get list of Pods using kubectl get pods
Use kubetail with Pod name prefix to tail multiple Pods
Try tailing specific containers with -c flag
Use regex patterns for advanced Pod matching
Filter logs by namespace using -n flag
hints: On MacOS: brew tap johanhaleby/kubetail && brew install kubetail
On Linux: Clone the repo and add to your shell plugins
Kubetail matches Pod names by prefix, so 'kubetail app' matches all Pods starting with 'app'
Use --regex flag for advanced pattern matching
Combine -c flags to tail multiple specific containers

editor_config.initial_code:
#!/bin/bash

# ========================================
# KUBETAIL INSTALLATION AND USAGE GUIDE
# ========================================

# INSTALLATION
# ============

# MacOS Installation
brew tap johanhaleby/kubetail && brew install kubetail

# Linux Installation
# git clone https://github.com/johanhaleby/kubetail.git kubetail
# Add to ~/.zshrc: plugins=( ... kubetail )
# source ~/.zshrc

# VERIFICATION
# ============

# Check kubetail is installed
which kubetail

# View help
kubetail -h

# BASIC USAGE EXAMPLES
# ====================

# 1. Get list of Pods
kubectl get pods

# 2. Tail logs from Pods with prefix "app2"
kubetail app2

# 3. Tail specific container
kubetail app2 -c container1

# 4. Tail multiple containers
kubetail app2 -c container1 -c container2

# 5. Tail multiple applications
kubetail app1,app2

# 6. Use regex for pattern matching
kubetail "^app1|.*my-demo.*" --regex

# 7. Tail within specific namespace
kubetail app2 -n production

# KUBECTL ALTERNATIVE
# ===================

# Using kubectl with label selectors
kubectl logs --selector=app=my-app
kubectl logs --selector=app=my-app,environment=production

# Follow logs with kubectl (single Pod)
kubectl logs -f pod-name

# Follow logs for specific container
kubectl logs -f pod-name -c container-name

# REAL-WORLD SCENARIOS
# ====================

# Scenario 1: Monitor all nginx replicas
kubetail nginx

# Scenario 2: Debug sidecar logs across all app Pods
kubetail myapp -c log-shipper

# Scenario 3: Monitor staging environment
kubetail myapp -n staging

# Scenario 4: Follow logs matching multiple patterns
kubetail "frontend|backend" --regex

# Scenario 5: Production monitoring with multiple containers
kubetail myapp -c app -c sidecar -n production
